annotations.label: Definition of π as ratio of circumference to diameter

anchor.snippet: π is commonly defined as the ratio of a circle 's circumference C to its diameter d

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Real.pi` analytically (twice the smallest zero of cos), not as the circumference/diameter ratio of a geometric circle.

anchors.snippet: π is commonly defined as the ratio of a circle 's circumference C to its diameter d

annotations.label: Weierstrass integral definition of π

anchor.snippet: An integral such as this was proposed as a definition of π by Karl Weierstrass

annotations.note: Not used as a definition, but `∫_{-1}^{1} √(1-x²) dx = π/2` is proved as `integral_sqrt_one_sub_sq`.

annotations.label: Baltzer–Landau definition via cosine

anchor.snippet: π is twice the smallest positive number at which the cosine function equals 0

annotations.note: This is exactly how `Real.pi` is defined in Mathlib: `2 * Classical.choose exists_cos_eq_zero` on `[1,2]`.

annotations.label: Definition via complex exponential

anchor.snippet: π can be defined using properties of the complex exponential

annotations.note: Mathlib's π is defined via real cosine rather than as the half-period of `exp`, although `Complex.exp_pi_mul_I` connects the two.

anchors.snippet: π can be defined using properties of the complex exponential

annotations.label: π is irrational

anchor.snippet: π is an irrational number , meaning that it cannot be written as the ratio of two integers

annotations.note: Statement and proof are in `irrational_pi : Irrational π`.

annotations.label: Normality conjecture for π

anchor.snippet: The conjecture that π is normal has not been proven or disproven

annotations.note: An open conjecture; no normality statement for π exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: π is transcendental (Lindemann–Weierstrass)

anchor.snippet: π is also a transcendental number

annotations.note: No `Transcendental ℤ π` or Lindemann–Weierstrass result is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Impossibility of squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: it is not possible to " square the circle "

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks the constructibility framework for compass-and-straightedge impossibility of squaring the circle.

annotations.label: Simple continued fraction of π

anchor.snippet: π can be represented by an infinite series of nested fractions, called a simple continued fraction

annotations.note: Mathlib has general continued-fraction theory but no specific continued-fraction expansion of π.

annotations.label: Best rational approximations from continued fraction

anchor.snippet: Each approximation generated in this way is a best rational approximation

annotations.note: Mathlib has the general best-rational-approximation theorem for continued fractions, not applied to π specifically.

annotations.label: Polar representation of complex numbers

anchor.snippet: Any complex number , say z , can be expressed using a pair of real numbers

annotations.note: `polarCoord` (and `Complex.arg`/`Complex.abs`) give the polar representation `z = r·exp(iθ)`.

annotations.label: Euler's formula

anchor.snippet: described by Euler's formula

annotations.note: `Complex.exp_mul_I : exp (θ * I) = cos θ + sin θ * I` is Euler's formula.

annotations.label: Euler's identity

anchor.snippet: results in Euler's identity

annotations.note: `Complex.exp_pi_mul_I : exp (π * I) = -1` is exactly Euler's identity.

annotations.label: nth roots of unity

anchor.snippet: and these are called the " n th roots of unity "

annotations.note: `Complex.isPrimitiveRoot_exp` shows `exp(2πi/n)` is a primitive nth root of unity, with `rootsOfUnity` framework.

annotations.label: Archimedes' polygon bounds on π

anchor.snippet: By calculating the perimeters of these polygons, he proved that

annotations.note: Mathlib has rigorous numeric bounds on π in `Real.Pi.Bounds` but not Archimedes' specific polygon-perimeter argument.

annotations.label: Viète's formula

anchor.snippet: François Viète published what is now known as Viète's formula

annotations.note: Viète's infinite-product formula for 2/π is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wallis product

anchor.snippet: John Wallis published what is now known as the Wallis product

annotations.note: `Real.tendsto_prod_pi_div_two` is exactly the Wallis product.

annotations.label: Gregory–Leibniz arctangent series

anchor.snippet: discovered the Taylor series expansion for arctangent

annotations.note: `Real.tendsto_sum_pi_div_four` formalises arctan(1) = π/4 via the alternating odd-reciprocal series.

annotations.label: Machin's formula

anchor.snippet: John Machin used the Gregory–Leibniz series to produce an algorithm that converged much faster

annotations.note: Machin's identity `π/4 = 4 arctan(1/5) − arctan(1/239)` is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Newton's accelerated arctan series

anchor.snippet: Isaac Newton accelerated the convergence of the Gregory–Leibniz series in 1684

annotations.note: Newton's accelerated arctan series for π is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gregory–Leibniz series for π

anchor.snippet: A simple infinite series for π is the Gregory–Leibniz series

annotations.note: Same theorem as the named result; converges to π/4.

annotations.label: Nilakantha's series for π

anchor.snippet: An infinite series for π (published by Nilakantha in the 15th century) that converges more rapidly than the Gregory–Leibniz series

annotations.note: Nilakantha's particular series `3 + 4/(2·3·4) − 4/(4·5·6) + …` is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Basel problem solution (ζ(2) = π²/6)

anchor.snippet: When Euler solved the Basel problem in 1735, finding the exact value of the sum of the reciprocal squares

annotations.note: `hasSum_zeta_two : HasSum (fun n => 1/n^2) (π^2/6)`.

annotations.label: Lambert's proof that π is irrational

anchor.snippet: Swiss scientist Johann Heinrich Lambert in 1768 proved that π is irrational

annotations.note: The statement is formalized as `irrational_pi`, though Mathlib's proof follows Niven rather than Lambert.

annotations.label: Legendre: π² is irrational

anchor.snippet: French mathematician Adrien-Marie Legendre proved in 1794 that π 2 is also irrational

annotations.note: Mathlib has `irrational_pi` but not the stronger `Irrational (π^2)`.

annotations.label: Lindemann's proof that π is transcendental

anchor.snippet: German mathematician Ferdinand von Lindemann proved that π is transcendental

annotations.note: Transcendence of π is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauss–Legendre iterative algorithm

anchor.snippet: The Gauss–Legendre iterative algorithm

annotations.note: The Gauss–Legendre AGM-based algorithm for π is not formalized.

annotations.label: Ramanujan's series for π

anchor.snippet: One of his formulae, based on modular equations

annotations.note: Ramanujan's rapidly convergent series for 1/π are not formalized.

annotations.label: Chudnovsky formula

anchor.snippet: The Chudnovsky formula developed in 1987

annotations.note: `chudnovskySum` defines the series but the equality with `π⁻¹` is left as a `proof_wanted`.

annotations.label: Plouffe's PSLQ template formulae

anchor.snippet: Simon Plouffe used the PSLQ integer relation algorithm

annotations.note: PSLQ-discovered template formulae for π are not formalized.

annotations.label: Buffon's needle approximation of π

anchor.snippet: Buffon's needle is one such technique

annotations.note: Proved in the Archive as `buffon_short`/`buffon_long`, giving the needle-crossing probability `2l/(πd)`.

annotations.label: Dots-in-square Monte Carlo method

anchor.snippet: Another Monte Carlo method for computing π is to draw a circle inscribed in a square

annotations.note: The inscribed-circle dot-counting Monte Carlo estimator for π is not formalized.

annotations.label: Random walk computation of π

anchor.snippet: Another way to calculate π using probability is to start with a random walk

annotations.note: No random-walk-based estimator for π is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BBP digit extraction formula

anchor.snippet: the BBP digit extraction algorithm , was discovered in 1995 by Simon Plouffe

annotations.note: The Bailey–Borwein–Plouffe formula is not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the BBP digit extraction algorithm , was discovered in 1995 by Simon Plouffe

annotations.label: Circumference of a circle

anchor.snippet: The circumference of a circle with radius r is 2 πr

annotations.note: There is no `circumference = 2πr` lemma; a notion of arc length of a circle is not directly available.

annotations.label: Area of a circle

anchor.snippet: The area of a circle with radius r is πr 2

annotations.note: `volume_ball_fin_two` gives `volume (ball x r) = r² · π`; `Complex.volume_ball` is the same.

annotations.label: Area of an ellipse

anchor.snippet: The area of an ellipse with semi-major axis a and semi-minor axis b is πab

annotations.note: No `area(ellipse) = π a b` lemma was found.

annotations.label: Volume of a sphere

anchor.snippet: The volume of a sphere with radius r is

annotations.note: `volume_ball_fin_three` gives `volume (ball x r) = r³ · 4π/3`.

annotations.label: Surface area of a sphere

anchor.snippet: The surface area of a sphere with radius r is 4 πr 2

annotations.note: No formalization of the 2-sphere surface area `4πr²` was found.

annotations.label: Barbier's theorem

anchor.snippet: By Barbier's theorem , every curve of constant width has perimeter π times its width

annotations.note: Barbier's theorem on curves of constant width is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integral for area of semicircle

anchor.snippet: an integral that specifies half the area of a circle of radius one

annotations.note: `integral_sqrt_one_sub_sq : ∫ x in -1..1, √(1-x²) = π/2`.

annotations.label: Radian measure with π

anchor.snippet: a complete circle spans an angle of 2 π radians

annotations.note: `Real.Angle` is `ℝ ⧸ (2π·ℤ)`, encoding the full-turn = 2π radian convention but not a stand-alone definition lemma.

annotations.label: Periodicity of sine and cosine

anchor.snippet: sine and cosine have period 2 π

annotations.note: `Real.sin_periodic`/`Real.cos_periodic` give periodicity with period `2π`.

anchors.snippet: sine and cosine have period 2 π

annotations.label: Vibrating string eigenvalue problem

anchor.snippet: an idealized vibrating string can be modelled as the graph of a function f on the unit interval

annotations.note: The vibrating string eigenvalue / Dirichlet Laplacian problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Wirtinger's inequality

anchor.snippet: which satisfies Wirtinger's inequality

annotations.note: Wirtinger's inequality (π as best Poincaré constant on `[0,1]`) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Isoperimetric inequality

anchor.snippet: the best constant in the isoperimetric inequality

annotations.note: The isoperimetric inequality with the sharp `4π` constant is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Critical Sobolev inequality in two dimensions

anchor.snippet: In two dimensions, the critical Sobolev inequality is

annotations.note: The 2D critical Sobolev inequality is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Poincaré inequality with π as best constant

anchor.snippet: π is the greatest constant such that

annotations.note: Sharp Poincaré inequality with constant π is not formalized.

annotations.label: Fourier transform

anchor.section: Fourier transform and Heisenberg uncertainty principle
anchor.snippet: This is the integral transform , that takes a complex-valued integrable function f on the real line

annotations.note: `Real.fourierIntegral` (and the more general `VectorFourier.fourierIntegral`) defines the Fourier transform with the `e^{-2πi x ξ}` convention.

annotations.label: Heisenberg uncertainty principle

anchor.section: Fourier transform and Heisenberg uncertainty principle
anchor.snippet: The uncertainty principle gives a sharp lower bound on the extent to which it is possible to localize a function both in space and in frequency

annotations.note: No formalization of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle was found.

annotations.label: Gaussian function (normal distribution density)

anchor.snippet: The Gaussian function , which is the probability density function of the normal distribution

annotations.note: `gaussianPDFReal μ v x = (1/√(2π v))·exp(-(x-μ)²/(2v))` is the normal density.

annotations.label: Gaussian integral

anchor.snippet: This follows from a change of variables in the Gaussian integral

annotations.note: `integral_gaussian : ∫ x, exp(-b x²) = √(π/b)`.

annotations.label: Gaussian is its own Fourier transform

anchor.snippet: π is the unique constant making the Gaussian normal distribution

annotations.note: `fourier_gaussian_pi` shows the suitably normalised Gaussian equals its own Fourier transform.

annotations.label: Gauss–Bonnet formula

anchor.snippet: The constant π appears in the Gauss–Bonnet formula which relates the differential geometry of surfaces to their topology

annotations.note: The Gauss–Bonnet theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cauchy's integral formula

anchor.snippet: A form of Cauchy's integral formula states that if a point z 0 is interior to γ

annotations.note: Cauchy's integral formula for a disk is `Complex.two_pi_I_inv_smul_circleIntegral_sub_inv_smul_of_differentiable_on_off_countable`.

annotations.label: Cauchy's integral formula (general form)

anchor.snippet: The general form of Cauchy's integral formula establishes the relationship

annotations.note: Mathlib has the disk-version higher-derivative coefficients via `cauchyPowerSeries`, but not the general curve/winding-number form.

annotations.label: Residue theorem

anchor.snippet: Cauchy's integral formula is a special case of the residue theorem

annotations.note: No general residue theorem (sum of residues weighted by winding numbers) was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-dimensional Newtonian potential

anchor.snippet: the two-dimensional Newtonian potential , representing the potential of a point source at the origin

annotations.note: Newtonian potentials in `ℝ²` are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three-dimensional Newtonian potential

anchor.snippet: in three dimensions, the Newtonian potential is

annotations.note: The 3D Newtonian potential `1/(4π r)` is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Total curvature of a smooth plane curve

anchor.snippet: the total curvature of a smooth plane curve is the amount it turns anticlockwise, in radians, from start to finish

annotations.note: No definition of total curvature of a plane curve was found.

annotations.label: Total curvature of closed curve equals 2πN

anchor.snippet: For a closed curve, this quantity is equal to

annotations.note: The Hopf Umlaufsatz / total curvature = 2π N statement is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gamma function at half-integers contains π

anchor.snippet: When the gamma function is evaluated at half-integers, the result contains π

annotations.note: `Real.Gamma_one_half_eq : Γ(1/2) = √π`, with `Gamma_nat_add_half` for general half-integers.

annotations.label: Weierstrass product for the gamma function

anchor.snippet: The gamma function is defined by its Weierstrass product development

annotations.note: Gamma is defined via the Euler integral (not the Weierstrass product); the Weierstrass-product formula itself is not proved.

annotations.label: Volume of n-ball and surface area of (n−1)-sphere

anchor.snippet: The gamma function is used to calculate the volume

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.volume_ball` gives `r^n · √π^n / Γ(n/2+1)`; surface area of the sphere is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Stirling's approximation

anchor.snippet: which is known as Stirling's approximation

annotations.note: `tendsto_stirlingSeq_sqrt_pi` gives `n!/(√n (n/e)^n) → √π`, i.e. Stirling's asymptotic.

annotations.label: Ehrhart's volume conjecture

anchor.snippet: Ehrhart's volume conjecture is that this is the (optimal) upper bound on the volume of a convex body

annotations.note: Ehrhart's volume conjecture is an open conjecture and is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Basel problem: ζ(2) = π²/6

anchor.section: Number theory and Riemann zeta function
anchor.snippet: Finding a simple solution for this infinite series was a famous problem in mathematics called the Basel problem

annotations.note: Same as the earlier entry: `hasSum_zeta_two` formalizes ζ(2) = π²/6.

annotations.label: Probability two random integers are coprime

anchor.section: Number theory and Riemann zeta function
anchor.snippet: the probability of two random numbers being relatively prime

annotations.note: The density-`6/π²` statement on coprime pairs of integers is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemann's functional equation

anchor.section: Number theory and Riemann zeta function
anchor.snippet: The zeta function also satisfies Riemann's functional equation

annotations.note: `completedRiemannZeta_one_sub : Λ(1-s) = Λ(s)` (with the asymmetric `riemannZeta_one_sub` variant also available).

annotations.label: Derivative of zeta function identity

anchor.section: Number theory and Riemann zeta function
anchor.snippet: Furthermore, the derivative of the zeta function satisfies

annotations.note: No closed-form identity for ζ'(s) of the type quoted on Wikipedia was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characters of the circle group

anchor.snippet: It is a theorem that every character of T is one of the complex exponentials

annotations.note: Mathlib has `AddCircle.toCircle` and Pontryagin duality machinery, but I did not find a theorem stating that every character of `T` is one of the integer exponentials.

annotations.label: Bourbaki's definition of π

anchor.snippet: A definition of the constant, due to Nicolas Bourbaki , takes π to be half the magnitude of the derivative of this isomorphism

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the cosine-root definition rather than Bourbaki's derivative-of-`ℝ/ℤ`-isomorphism definition.

annotations.label: Jacobi theta function

anchor.snippet: An example is the Jacobi theta function

annotations.note: `jacobiTheta` is the one-variable Jacobi theta, with the two-variable `jacobiTheta₂` also available.

annotations.label: Theta function automorphic transformation

anchor.snippet: The constant π is the unique constant making the Jacobi theta function an automorphic form

annotations.note: `jacobiTheta_S_smul` is the modular S-transformation `θ(-1/τ) = (-iτ)^{1/2} θ(τ)`.

annotations.label: Cauchy distribution

anchor.snippet: The Cauchy distribution

annotations.note: `cauchyPDFReal x₀ γ x = π⁻¹ γ / ((x-x₀)² + γ²)`.

annotations.label: Shannon entropy of the Cauchy distribution

anchor.snippet: The Shannon entropy of the Cauchy distribution is equal to

annotations.note: Shannon entropy of the Cauchy distribution is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hilbert transform

anchor.snippet: The Hilbert transform H is the integral transform given by the Cauchy principal value of the singular integral

annotations.note: The Hilbert transform is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: π in the Mandelbrot set

anchor.snippet: An occurrence of π in the fractal called the Mandelbrot set was discovered by David Boll in 1991

annotations.note: The Mandelbrot set itself is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Period of a simple pendulum

anchor.snippet: A simple formula from the field of classical mechanics gives the approximate period T of a simple pendulum

annotations.note: Classical-mechanics pendulum-period formula is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle (quantum mechanics)

anchor.snippet: One of the key formulae of quantum mechanics is Heisenberg's uncertainty principle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no quantum-mechanics uncertainty formulation.

annotations.label: Orthopositronium inverse lifetime

anchor.snippet: The inverse lifetime to lowest order in the fine-structure constant

annotations.note: Particle-physics formulas like the orthopositronium lifetime are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euler's buckling formula

anchor.snippet: the buckling formula derived by Euler, which gives the maximum axial load F

annotations.note: Euler's beam-buckling formula is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stokes' law

anchor.snippet: The field of fluid dynamics contains π in Stokes' law

annotations.note: Stokes' law of fluid drag is not in Mathlib.
